analogy: Think of "Rulers and Buildings" through the chapter's own opening images: 49 TRIBES, NOMADS AND SETTLED COMMUNITIES You saw in Chapters 2, 3 and 4 how kingdoms rose and fell. Even as this was happening, new arts, and villages. Over the centuries important political, social and economic developments had taken place. But social change was not... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 49 TRIBES, NOMADS AND, SETTLED COMMUNITIES, TRIBES, NOMADS AND, 51 TRIBES, NOMADS AND. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Rulers and Buildings is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 49 TRIBES, NOMADS AND SETTLED COMMUNITIES You saw in Chapters 2, 3 and 4 how kingdoms rose and fell.

The text explains that even as this was happening, new arts, and villages.

The text explains that over the centuries important political, social and economic developments had taken place.

The text explains that but social change was not the same everywhere, because different kinds of societies evolved differently.

The text explains that it is important to understand how and why this happened.

The text explains that in large parts of the subcontinent, society was already divided according to the rules of .

The text explains that these rules, as prescribed by the Brahmanas, were accepted by the rulers of large kingdoms.

The text explains that the difference between the high and low, and between the rich and poor, increased.

The text explains that under the Delhi Sultans and the Mughals, this hierarchy between social classes grew further.

The text explains that beyond Big Cities: Tribal Societies There were, however, other kinds of societies as well.

The text explains that many societies in the subcontinent did not follow the social rules and rituals prescribed by the Brahmanas.

The text explains that nor were they divided into numerous unequal classes.

The text explains that 50 Members of each tribe were united by kinship bonds.

The text explains that many tribes obtained their livelihood from agriculture.

The text explains that most often they combined these activities to make full use of the natural resources of the area in which they lived.

The text explains that some tribes were nomadic and moved from one place to another.

The text explains that sultan Alauddin Khalji (Chapter 3) used the Banjaras to transport grain to the city markets.

The text explains that changing Society: New Castes and Hierarchies As the economy and the needs of society grew, people with new skills were required.

The text explains that on the other hand, many tribes and social groups were taken into caste-based society and given the status of .

The text explains that specialised artisans – smiths, carpenters and masons – were also recognised as separate by the Brahmanas. , rather than , became the basis for organising society.

The text explains that deliberations on jati A twelfth-century inscription from Uyyakondan Udaiyar, in Tiruchirapalli taluka (in present-day Tamil Nadu), describes the deliberations in a sabha (Chapter 2) of Brahmanas.

The text explains that here a developed society was emerging, and rulers used their wealth to create powerful states.

The text explains that gradually, with the support of the Brahmanas, many tribes became a part of the caste system.

The text explains that a large majority joined the lower of caste society.

The text explains that two examples of this important part of our history are described below.

The text explains that the , a history of Akbar’s reign, mentions the Gond kingdom of Garha Katanga that had 70,000 villages.

The text explains that the administrative system of these kingdoms was becoming centralised.

The text explains that the emergence of large states changed the nature of Gond society.

The text explains that their basically equal society gradually got divided into unequal social classes.

The text explains that they created a new state by suppressing the older political system of the (landlords).

The text explains that but direct Mughal control over the region could not last long.

The text explains that at other times, they were engaged in building dams, irrigation systems and other public works.

The text explains that conclusion Considerable social change took place in the subcontinent during the period we have been examining. -based society and tribal people constantly interacted with each other.

The text explains that over a period of time, many of them merged with caste-based society.

The text explains that others, however, rejected both the caste system and orthodox Hinduism.

The text explains that some tribes established extensive states with well-organised systems of administration.

The text explains that in what ways was the history of the Gonds different from that of the Ahoms?

The text explains that plot the location of the tribes mentioned in this chapter on a map.

The text explains that find out about present-day government policies towards tribal populations and organise a discussion about these.

The text explains that 60 (d) The Bhils lived in the north-eastern part of the subcontinent.

The text explains that what kinds of exchanges took place between nomadic pastoralists and settled agriculturists?

The text explains that how was the administration of the Ahom state organised?

The text explains that how did tribal societies change after being organised into a state?

The text explains that for any two, discuss whether their mode of livelihood was suited to the geography and the environment of the area where they lived.

The text explains that find out more about present-day nomadic pastoral groups in the subcontinent.

The text explains that which are the areas frequented by these groups?

Source: NCERT Class 7 Our Pasts II, Chapter 5: Rulers and Buildings

cases.name: Their
cases.point: Their chief, Kamal Khan Gakkhar, was made a noble ( ) by Emperor Akbar. (NCERT Class 7 Our Pasts II Ch5 pg 1)

cases.name: In Multan
cases.point: In Multan and Sind, the Langahs and Arghuns dominated extensive regions before they were subdued by the Mughals. (NCERT Class 7 Our Pasts II Ch5 pg 1)

cases.name: The Balochis
cases.point: The Balochis were another large and powerful On a physical map of the subcontinent, identify the areas in which tribal people may have lived. ? (NCERT Class 7 Our Pasts II Ch5 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 49 TRIBES, NOMADS AND and SETTLED COMMUNITIES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Rulers and Buildings; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Rulers and Buildings through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 49 TRIBES, NOMADS AND and SETTLED COMMUNITIES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
